_provenance: fan-out:wf_d565ece0 (haiku research + sonnet verify + sonnet score); enriched 2026-06-20 with magazine sail tests, builder interviews, owner accounts, and brokerage listings
name: Boréal 47.2

facts.value: Lead ballast cast as moulded blocks (roughly 450 kg each) and bedded in resin at the lowest point of a shallow keel embryo (a stub keel that also protects the rudder when beaching). Total ballast about 3,800 kg. Boréal places all heavy items low and amidships, which improves both stability and motion. The original Boréal 47 carried a ballast ratio near 36 percent; the heavier 47.2 hull lowers that figure toward the high-20s/low-30s, a point the design press has flagged.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
Interesting Sailboats: THE NEW BOREAL 44.2

facts.value: Light displacement of the original Boréal 47 is about 10,700 kg (23,600 lb); loaded/half-load figures quoted by reviewers run to roughly 11,300-12,400 kg. The 47.2 hull is heavier than the 47. Displacement/length is about 193, which is moderate for a cruising boat of this size.

sources.title: Boréal 47 - Boat-Specs.com
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)

facts.value: CE Category A (Ocean), the highest design category, certifying the boat for sustained winds over Force 8 and significant wave heights above 4 m.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

facts.value: No Angle of Vanishing Stability is published by Boréal or quoted in any magazine test located. Reviewers describe the boat as stiff and resistant to extra heel in gusts, but that is a qualitative impression, not a measured righting-moment curve. An honest buyer should treat the boat's ultimate-stability envelope as undocumented and ask the builder directly for an ISO 12217 stability calculation on a specific hull and loadout.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: No STIX value is published in any accessible source. The boat is CE Category A certified (which requires a STIX of at least 32 under ISO 12217), but the actual figure is not disclosed.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

facts.value: Refleks diesel drip-stove heating with hot-water circulation to radiators in the cabins, plus blowers in the heads. Owners who have cruised Chile, South Georgia and Greenland praise it specifically; one Boréal 47 owner said they 'could not have sailed in Chile and South Georgia without heat, or at least we would have been miserable.' The radiators are quiet and the blowers can dry several sets of wet foul-weather gear in an evening.

sources.title: Boréal 47 - An Owner's Experience - Attainable Adventure Cruising
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)

facts.value: Roughly 80 mm (8 cm) of insulation throughout the hull and deck. A sprayed cork layer is applied to the aluminium frames before the foam panels go on, which co-founder Jean-François Eeman says is done specifically to break thermal bridges and stop condensation. The result is verified by owners in sub-10 C overnight temperatures and in high-latitude winters.

sources.title: An expedition into the world of Boreal - no-frills-sailing.com (interview with Jean-François Eeman)
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

facts.value: A heavy aluminium compression post carries the rig loads down through the saloon, with the centreboard trunk behind it. Whether the Sparcraft mast itself is technically deck-stepped or keel-stepped is not stated unambiguously in any accessible source; the load path through the post and the beachable shallow-draft design are what matter in practice.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

facts.value: Full collision bulkheads forward and aft create watertight compartments at each end of the hull. Bottom plating is a heavy 10 mm in the keel and bottom area, tapering to 6 mm at the transom and 4 mm in the deck and coachroof. The shallow keel embryo and stout bottom let the boat take the ground or be deliberately beached.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

facts.value: The signature feature is a hard aluminium pilothouse (the 'doghouse') with a watertight door, an inside nav/watch seat at cockpit level, and a near 360-degree view. Crew can keep watch in the dry and warm and only step out to handle sails. On the Sir Ernst Boréal 47 in 50-knot Antarctic winds the skipper stayed 'dry in the fantastic doghouse, enjoying a stunning 360 degree view.' Steering itself is from an open cockpit at the forward end, behind a hard dodger.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)
SIR ERNST: A YouTube-Gem of real sailors - no-frills-sailing.com

facts.value: A large Dorade-style vent is built into the aft edge of the double-walled deckhouse and keeps drawing air below even in severe weather with the hatches shut. Boréal markets the design as working equally well in the tropics and at high latitudes, and the pilothouse windows open for saloon airflow.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT
Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: Cockpit shade is an optional external system rather than a standard fixture; the hard dodger over the forward cockpit gives fixed shelter for two. The deck layout is marketed as comfortable in both the tropics and high latitudes, but a tropical buyer should budget for a proper bimini or awning.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

facts.value: A large two-drawer refrigerator is standard in the starboard galley. A separate freezer can be added as an option, commonly fitted in the starboard aft cabin space, rather than by converting a fridge drawer.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: A watermaker is not standard, but the boat is delivered with pre-installation provision for one alongside the optional diesel generator, and the systems space is sized to take both. Owners fitting one report the months-of-autonomy that the boat is designed for. Practically every serious tropical or expedition buyer fits a watermaker after purchase.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: The doghouse with its full-width Dorade vent, opening pilothouse windows and deck hatches gives good passive cross-flow. Dual foot pumps deliver both saltwater and pressurised freshwater at the galley, which helps conserve tank water in warm climates.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: No factory windvane is offered. The boat has a single protected centreline rudder and a pair of epoxy-composite daggerboards set well aft; adjusting the daggerboards lets the crew trim the helm toward neutral, which is unusually good for an autopilot and helps a self-steering setup. A windvane can be retrofitted on the reverse transom but is an owner project.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)

facts.value: The boat's helm balance makes it exceptionally easy to steer hands-off. On the Cruising World test the boat under reefed main and partly furled genoa held its course unaided for several minutes with no autopilot engaged. No single autopilot brand is standard; brokerage examples show NKE and other systems fitted to owner choice.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

facts.value: Electrical fit-out is highly owner-specific. The boat provides pre-installation for an optional diesel generator, and brokerage examples show large AGM banks (for instance four 130 Ah AGM service batteries with a Mastervolt charger and a Superwind wind generator on a 2013 boat). More recent boats have been specified with up to roughly 1,100 W of solar and around 800 Ah of lithium. Batteries sit low and central for weight distribution.

sources.title: BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers
Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: Reverse transom with a fold-down bathing platform and a dedicated transom locker for liferaft stowage. The aft deck is clear and gives safe passage forward.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: The 47.2 has slightly more freeboard and interior volume than the previous Boréal 47, but the design philosophy avoids piling on height purely to gain accommodation. Visibility from the pilothouse and helm is good.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

facts.value: Wide, uncluttered side decks let crew move fore and aft without big steps. The V1 (cap) shrouds land on the coachroof rather than the rail so they do not block the gangway, and there are welded-on cleats, double stanchions, and granny bars at the mast.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World
SIR ERNST: A YouTube-Gem of real sailors - no-frills-sailing.com

facts.value: A single, well-protected centreline rudder is simpler to manage in an emergency than twin rudders, and the daggerboards let the crew tune the helm to near neutral for hands-free heave-to. Granny bars at the mast give a secure stance when working forward, and the pilothouse gives a constantly manned, dry watch position. No model-specific MOB equipment is standard.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)

facts.value: Real high-latitude and heavy-weather accounts exist for the type. The Boréal 47 'Sir Ernst' sailed to the Antarctic and crossed both the Pacific and Atlantic; in 50-knot Antarctic winds an observer described the motion as 'so smooth, so superior' with the crew 'absolutely safe' and the skipper dry in the doghouse, and the channel documents a 72-knot gust reading on the South Georgia to Cape Town leg. SAIL's reviewer, sailing the boat in residual Hurricane Maria swell, called the motion 'at all times both easy and forgiving, with no slamming or whipping about, the definition of a seakindly boat.' These are firsthand observations, not survival-storm tactical reports, but they are consistent and credible.

sources.title: SIR ERNST: A YouTube-Gem of real sailors - no-frills-sailing.com
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

facts.value: A double-headsail rig: a furling genoa on the headstay and a self-tacking staysail (about 26 sq m) on an inner stay, both on Profurl furlers. Around 17 to 20 knots the crew rolls the genoa and works under staysail. A removable inner forestay carrying a hanked-on staysail is offered as an alternative to furling. The genoa and staysail are not flown together; the staysail can be deeply reefed for the worst conditions.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

facts.value: The deep keel embryo holds the lead ballast below the rudder, so the boat sits upright and stable when deliberately dried out or beached, and reviewers report it can recover from heel of roughly 14 degrees while aground without falling over. The shallow board-up draft (about 1.0 m) is what makes drying out and exploring thin water practical.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)

facts.value: Hard-chined hull in marine aluminium, using 5083 and 5086 H111 alloy from Norway. Plate is laser- or waterjet-cut to fit and welded, with thickness graded from 10 mm in the bottom and keel down to 4 mm in deck and coachroof. Below-waterline through-hulls are standpipes that rise above the waterline with the seacock on top, so seacocks can be removed and serviced with the boat afloat. The hull is sandblasted and epoxy barrier-coated within about eight hours of blasting, before oxidation can start.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)

facts.value: The centreboard is a hollow aluminium blade shaped as a NACA foil for windward performance, drawing about 2.5 m down and retracting into a trunk for shallow water. Brokerage detail describes it with a roughly 10 mm internal structure and 8 mm skin, ribbed and pivoting on an Ertalon (engineering plastic) shaft about 120 mm in diameter. The two daggerboards set aft are epoxy composite, not aluminium, so they break away rather than damage the hull if struck.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

facts.value: Generous expedition tankage. Magazine specs give roughly 760 litres (200 US gal) fresh water and 600 litres (158 US gal) diesel; one 2013 brokerage boat lists 636 litres water, 594 litres fuel, plus separate black-water (about 40 L) and grey-water tanks. Fuel for around 600 litres gives long motoring range for calms and high-latitude glacier work, and the diesel feeds both the engine and the Refleks heater.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

facts.value: Volvo Penta D2 diesel on a shaft, offered across the production run from about 50 hp up to 75 hp (50, 55, 60 and 70/75 hp variants appear on different boats and brokerage listings); a Nanni N4.60 has also been offered. A 2013 boat shows the 55 hp D2-55F with under 2,000 hours. Three-blade reversible (feathering-style) propeller.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

facts.value: The Boréal 47.2 is expensive and largely built to order, with an 18 to 24 month wait. Magazine reporting puts the base price near 987,000 euros gross (including VAT), well over a million US dollars once equipped. The earlier Boréal 47 sold new around 453,000 euros in 2016 and around 555,000 to 650,000 US dollars at 2018 US reviews. Used boats are scarce: recent listings have included a Boréal 47 around 599,000 US dollars in the US and a larger Boréal 52 near 885,000 euros at a European broker, so depreciation is shallow and the used market is thin.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
Boreal 47.2 Narval and Boreal 52 Bravo listings - Berthon International

facts.value: No Boréal-specific rigging-replacement schedule is published. The boat carries a Sparcraft aluminium mast with stainless wire standing rigging, so the usual offshore practice applies: inspect closely from about 10 years and plan full wire replacement around 10 to 15 years, sooner if the boat has done hard high-latitude miles.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

facts.value: No major recurring structural defect shows up in owner accounts or professional reviews. The honest watch items are aluminium-specific and centreboard-specific. Bare aluminium holds up very well: one owner reports unpainted hull and chainplates uncorroded after 10 years, but painted surfaces are the weak point, with deck paint starting to need real attention after about six years and painted chainplates corroding badly where stainless toggles meet them. Galvanic protection (anodes, isolation of dissimilar metals, a hull-potential meter) must be maintained. The centreboard trunk and pivot need periodic inspection for wear from the blade working under load.

sources.title: Boréal 47 - An Owner's Experience - Attainable Adventure Cruising
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)

facts.value: Boréal builds in Tréguier, France, and uses mainstream gear: Volvo Penta (with Nanni as an alternative) diesels, Sparcraft aluminium spars, and Profurl furlers, all well supported worldwide. Aluminium construction means damage is repaired by welding, which is fixable almost anywhere there is a competent welder, but specialised parts such as Refleks stove spares are Scandinavian-sourced and worth carrying for remote cruising.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

praise: European Yacht of the Year 2021 winner in the Bluewater Cruiser category
Genuinely proven in the highest latitudes: owner boats have cruised Patagonia, Chile, South Georgia, Greenland, Iceland and the Antarctic
Seakindly motion confirmed by a magazine reviewer in residual hurricane swell and by an owner crew in 50-knot Antarctic winds
Outstanding cold-weather package: 80mm insulation with cork-sprayed frames eliminates condensation, and the Refleks diesel heater is widely loved for warmth and drying wet gear
Hard pilothouse doghouse lets the crew keep watch warm and dry with a near 360-degree view
Tough, repairable aluminium hull in quality Norwegian 5083/5086 alloy with graded plating up to 10mm in the bottom
Beachable shallow-draft design (about 1.0m board up) opens up anchorages and drying-out spots most deep-keel boats cannot reach
Exceptional helm balance: the boat will self-steer hands-off under sail, and daggerboards let you tune the helm to neutral
Full collision bulkheads fore and aft and standpipe through-hulls serviceable afloat reflect serious expedition engineering
Generous expedition tankage (around 760L water, 600L fuel) for long autonomy
Designed by Jean-François Delvoye out of a six-year family circumnavigation including long spells in Patagonia
complaints: Very expensive new (base near 987,000 euros) with an 18 to 24 month build wait
Thin used market and shallow depreciation, so finding a good used example is hard and rarely cheap
No factory windvane option; a self-steering vane is an owner retrofit
Aluminium needs ongoing corrosion discipline, and painted surfaces in particular (decks, chainplates) need attention from around year six
Watermaker and generator are options, not standard, so a passage-ready boat needs significant fit-out spend on top of the base price
The double-headsail rig swaps genoa for staysail rather than flying both, which is slightly less flexible than a true cutter
Reviewers note the design press has flagged the heavier hull's lower ballast ratio, so buyers should ask for an actual stability calculation rather than rely on reputation
known_issues: Painted aluminium surfaces (deck paint, painted chainplates) degrade faster than bare metal; deck paint needs real maintenance from around six years
Centreboard trunk and pivot wear from the blade working under sail load; periodic inspection needed
Galvanic corrosion risk if dissimilar metals are not properly isolated or anodes are neglected; managed by the factory anode and isolation system
Profurl furler foil can dent and jam a headsail if damaged; foil comes in replaceable sections
Daggerboard handling has a learning curve, since helm balance depends on getting the offset right

forums.title: OVNI vs Garcia vs Boreal comparison - YBW Forum
Aluminium Centreboard Boats discussion - YBW Forum
Boréal 47 - An Owner's Experience (Attainable Adventure Cruising)
Test Sail and Review of The Boréal 47.2 (Attainable Adventure Cruising)
SIR ERNST: A YouTube-Gem of real sailors (no-frills-sailing.com)

refit_items.action: Inspect from 10 years; budget full wire replacement around 10 to 15 years, or sooner after hard high-latitude miles. End fittings, toggles and furler-stay terminals renewed with the wire.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

refit_items.system: Aluminium hull - epoxy barrier coat and antifoul
refit_items.action: Below the waterline, refresh the epoxy barrier coat roughly every 5 to 7 years and antifoul over it; touch up any dings or scrapes to bare metal immediately to stop corrosion. Above the waterline, painted topsides and deck need periodic recoat (deck paint typically needs real attention from about year six).

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
Boréal 47 - An Owner's Experience - Attainable Adventure Cruising

refit_items.action: Replace the hull anode (around 5 kg), rudder anode and centreboard anode as they consume, typically every 2 to 3 years, and check the hull-potential meter regularly. Use zinc-aluminium anodes suited to an aluminium hull, not pure zinc.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)

refit_items.system: Centreboard pivot, lifting gear and trunk guides
refit_items.action: Inspect the centreboard pivot (Ertalon shaft), lifting cable/strop and trunk guide surfaces for wear from the blade working under load; renew worn guides, pivot bushing or strop as found.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)

refit_items.action: Replace any dented or bent foil section before it jams the headsail; foil comes in replaceable aluminium-sleeved sections.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

refit_items.action: Annual service: clean the burner pot, replace door and chimney seals, sweep the flue. Keep a service kit aboard for high-latitude trips since parts are Scandinavian-sourced.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)

refit_items.system: Volvo Penta D2 diesel and drivetrain
refit_items.action: Major service (oil, filters, coolant, hose and belt inspection) around every 500 hours; impeller, cutlass bearing and propeller shaft inspection at each haul-out. Long service life means overhaul, not repower, on a boat under about 20 years.

sources.title: BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

refit_items.action: Convert an aging AGM bank to LiFePO4 and add solar (recent boats have been specified with around 800 Ah lithium and roughly 1,100 W of solar). Optional generator pre-installation supports a watermaker and heavy electrical loads.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

refit_items.action: Fit an energy-appropriate watermaker into the provided pre-installation, paired with the generator if heavy output is wanted. Standard kit for any tropical or expedition program.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

refit_items.action: Refresh or replace UV-tired offshore sails. The double-headsail wardrobe is main, furling genoa and self-tacking staysail; a storm jib and trysail are worth having for high-latitude work.

sources.title: BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

refit_items.action: Liferaft service or replacement on its 5 to 6 year cycle, plus EPIRB battery, flares, jacklines, tethers and harnesses. The transom liferaft locker keeps it accessible.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

provisioning_specific.item: Marine aluminium offcuts (matching 5083/5086 alloy), aluminium-compatible epoxy filler and zinc-spray primer
provisioning_specific.why: This is an aluminium boat, so a grounding, dock strike or ice contact is repaired by welding, not fibreglass. Carrying offcuts in the same alloy as the hull lets you or a local welder make a proper field repair, and the primer/filler stops corrosion at any fresh scrape.

provisioning_specific.item: Zinc-aluminium sacrificial anode set (hull plate anode, rudder anode, centreboard anode)
provisioning_specific.why: The hull's galvanic protection depends on these anodes and they consume fastest at the hull. Correct zinc-aluminium anodes (not pure zinc, which can over-protect aluminium in warm saltwater) are hard to find in remote ports, so carry a full spare set.

provisioning_specific.item: Refleks stove service kit (burner pot, door and chimney seals, jet, flue brush)
provisioning_specific.why: The Refleks diesel heater is the heart of cold-weather comfort and the part owners single out for high-latitude cruising. Spares are Scandinavian-sourced and unavailable in most Pacific or Southern Ocean ports, so a full kit should be aboard for any high-latitude passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Centreboard spares: pivot bushing/shaft material (Ertalon or UHMW), spare lifting cable/strop and trunk guide plates
provisioning_specific.why: Centreboard trunk and pivot wear is the one boat-specific mechanical watch item. The shaft runs on engineering plastic and the lifting gear works under load; carrying bushing stock, a spare strop and guide material lets you fix board binding without hauling out.

provisioning_specific.item: Daggerboard adjustment fittings and locking pins (matched set)
provisioning_specific.why: Helm balance and the boat's superb hands-off steering depend on the aft daggerboards. The locking pins and adjustment cleats are small, boat-specific and not stocked in chandleries, so spares prevent losing the ability to trim the helm offshore.

provisioning_specific.item: Profurl furler foil section plus bearing/bushing kit
provisioning_specific.why: Both headsails furl on Profurl gear and a dented foil section can jam a sail. Sections are replaceable but must come from Profurl in France; one spare section per furler aboard prevents a stuck headsail in heavy weather.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo Penta D2 service kit: impeller, oil and fuel filters, belts, coolant, injector seals, spare zincs
provisioning_specific.why: The Volvo Penta D2 is well supported in major ports but not remote ones, and the engine also matters for charging and for feeding the Refleks heater fuel system. A full 500-hour service kit aboard avoids being engine-down in an anchorage with no chandlery.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker pre-filters and a spare membrane (if a watermaker is fitted)
provisioning_specific.why: On the months-of-autonomy program these boats are built for, the watermaker is critical and its consumables are the first thing to fail. Pre-filters and a spare membrane keep fresh water flowing far from any service agent.

provisioning_specific.item: Pilothouse door and hatch gasket material (EPDM/neoprene to suit the doghouse door profile)
provisioning_specific.why: The watertight doghouse door is the boat's primary dry, warm watch position; a failed gasket lets weather into the heated space. EPDM degrades with UV and age, so spare seal material is cheap insurance for a long high-latitude trip.

gaps: No published Angle of Vanishing Stability (AVS) for the Boréal 47 or 47.2; only qualitative 'sails stiff' impressions are available. Ask the builder for an ISO 12217 stability calculation on a specific hull and loadout.
No published STIX number, although CE Category A certification implies STIX of at least 32.
No measured righting-moment (GZ) curve available in any accessible source.
Loaded/half-load displacement figures vary between sources (roughly 11,300 to 12,400 kg); the builder's exact loaded displacement for the 47.2 is not pinned down.
Battery bank capacity and electrical architecture are highly owner-specific; no single standard fit-out is documented.
No published fuel-burn rate at cruise rpm for range planning under power.
Boréal-specific maintenance-interval schedule (rigging, centreboard, anodes) is not published; figures here use marine norms plus owner and reviewer reports.
Exact standard sail dimensions for the 47.2 (versus the earlier 47) are not separately published in accessible sources.

cleaned_facts.value: Lead ballast cast as moulded blocks (about 450 kg each) bedded in resin at the lowest point of a shallow keel embryo; total ballast about 3,800 kg. The original 47 ran a ballast ratio near 36 percent; the heavier 47.2 lowers it, a point reviewers have flagged.

On the Sir Ernst Boréal 47 in 50-knot Antarctic winds the skipper stayed dry in the doghouse. Steering is from an open cockpit behind a hard dodger.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)
SIR ERNST: A YouTube-Gem of real sailors - no-frills-sailing.com

cleaned_facts.value: A large Dorade-style vent in the aft edge of the double-walled deckhouse keeps drawing air below even in severe weather with hatches shut. Pilothouse windows open for saloon airflow.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

cleaned_facts.value: Cockpit shade is an optional external system; the hard dodger gives fixed shelter for two. A tropical buyer should budget for a bimini or awning.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

cleaned_facts.value: Large two-drawer fridge standard in the galley; a separate freezer is an option, commonly fitted in the starboard aft cabin space.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

cleaned_facts.value: Not standard, but delivered with pre-installation provision alongside the optional generator; the systems space takes both. Nearly all expedition/tropical buyers fit one after purchase.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

cleaned_facts.value: No factory windvane. Single centreline rudder with aft epoxy-composite daggerboards that tune helm balance toward neutral. A vane can be retrofitted on the reverse transom as an owner project.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

cleaned_facts.value: Helm balance makes hands-off steering easy; on the Cruising World test the boat held course unaided for several minutes with no autopilot. No single brand is standard; brokerage boats show systems such as NKE fitted to owner choice.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

cleaned_facts.value: Owner-specific. Pre-installation for an optional diesel generator; brokerage boats show large AGM banks (e.g. 4x130 Ah AGM, Mastervolt charger, Superwind wind generator on a 2013 boat), and recent boats up to roughly 800 Ah lithium with about 1,100 W solar. Batteries low and central.

sources.title: BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers
Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

cleaned_facts.value: Reverse transom with fold-down bathing platform and a dedicated transom locker for liferaft stowage.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

cleaned_facts.value: Wide, uncluttered side decks; no big steps fore and aft. Cap shrouds land on the coachroof so they do not block the gangway. Welded-on cleats, double stanchions, granny bars at the mast.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

cleaned_facts.value: Single protected centreline rudder simpler in emergency than twin rudders; daggerboards tune helm to near-neutral for hands-free heave-to; granny bars at the mast; constantly manned dry doghouse watch position.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT

cleaned_facts.value: Real high-latitude accounts exist: the Boréal 47 'Sir Ernst' sailed the Antarctic and crossed both oceans; in 50-knot Antarctic winds an observer described 'so smooth, so superior' motion with the crew safe and the skipper dry in the doghouse, with a 72-knot gust logged on the South Georgia to Cape Town leg. SAIL's reviewer in residual Hurricane Maria swell called the motion 'easy and forgiving... the definition of a seakindly boat.' Firsthand observations rather than survival-storm tactical reports.

sources.title: SIR ERNST: A YouTube-Gem of real sailors - no-frills-sailing.com
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

cleaned_facts.value: Double-headsail rig: furling genoa plus self-tacking staysail (about 26 sq m), both on Profurl furlers, with a removable inner forestay/hanked-staysail option. Genoa and staysail not flown together; staysail deeply reefable.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

cleaned_facts.value: Hard-chined hull in Norwegian 5083/5086 H111 marine aluminium, laser/waterjet-cut and welded, plating graded from 10 mm in the bottom/keel to 4 mm in the deck. Standpipe through-hulls let seacocks be serviced afloat. Hull epoxy barrier-coated within about 8 hours of sandblasting.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)

cleaned_facts.value: Hollow aluminium NACA-foil centreboard, about 2.5 m down, retracting into a trunk for shallow water; brokerage detail cites ~10 mm structure/8 mm skin on an Ertalon shaft about 120 mm diameter. Two aft daggerboards are epoxy composite so they break away if struck.

sources.title: BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

cleaned_facts.value: About 760 L (200 US gal) fresh water and 600 L (158 US gal) diesel per magazine specs; a 2013 brokerage boat lists 636 L water, 594 L fuel, plus separate black/grey-water tanks. Diesel feeds both engine and Refleks heater.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

cleaned_facts.value: Volvo Penta D2 diesel on a shaft, from about 50 hp to 75 hp across the run (Nanni N4.60 also offered). A 2013 boat shows the 55 hp D2-55F under 2,000 hours, three-blade reversible propeller.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
BOREAL 47 sailing yacht for sale - De Valk Yacht Brokers

cleaned_facts.value: Base price of the 47.2 near 987,000 euros gross (incl. VAT), 18-24 month build wait. The earlier 47 sold around 453,000 euros new in 2016 and 555,000-650,000 US dollars at 2018 US reviews. Used examples are scarce (a 47 around 599,000 US dollars in the US; a larger Boréal 52 near 885,000 euros at a European broker), so depreciation is shallow.

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test - YACHT
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser - Wave Train (Charles Doane)

cleaned_facts.value: No Boréal-specific schedule published. Sparcraft aluminium mast with stainless wire rigging; standard offshore practice is close inspection from 10 years and full wire replacement around 10-15 years, sooner after hard high-latitude miles.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - SAIL Magazine (Adam Cort)

cleaned_facts.value: No major recurring structural defect. Bare aluminium endures well (unpainted hull and chainplates uncorroded after 10 years per one owner) but painted surfaces are the weak point: deck paint needs attention from about year six, and painted chainplates corrode where stainless toggles meet them. Galvanic protection must be maintained; centreboard trunk/pivot need periodic wear inspection.

sources.title: Boréal 47 - An Owner's Experience - Attainable Adventure Cruising
Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)

cleaned_facts.value: Built in Tréguier, France, using mainstream gear (Volvo Penta with Nanni alternative, Sparcraft spars, Profurl furlers) supported worldwide. Aluminium damage is welded, repairable wherever there is a competent welder; specialised items such as Refleks spares are Scandinavian-sourced and worth carrying.

sources.title: Boat Review: Boreal 47 - Cruising World (Tim Murphy)
Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined - Yachting World

removed.key: stability.displacement (loaded figure: 17,000 kg with centerboard down)
removed.reason: No accessible source supports a 17,000 kg loaded figure, and the 'with centerboard down' qualifier is implausible (the board adds negligible displacement weight). Replaced with the sourced light-displacement figure (~10,700 kg) and the reviewer-quoted loaded range (~11,300-12,400 kg).

The precise '11 cm additional freeboard vs the previous 47' figure is unsupported by any accessible source. Replaced with the qualitative statement that the 47.2 has slightly more freeboard and volume than the 47.

Earlier draft asserted specific Windpilot/Aries owner installations; no accessible source confirms named installations. Replaced with the accurate statement that no factory vane is offered and a vane is an owner retrofit, supported by the daggerboard helm-balance description.

verify.verdict: Well supported after enrichment. The core facts - aluminium 5083/5086 hard-chine construction with graded plating, full collision bulkheads, lead ballast in a shallow keel embryo, Refleks diesel heating, ~80mm cork-and-foam insulation, the hard pilothouse doghouse, the double-headsail rig, the hollow-aluminium NACA centreboard with break-away epoxy daggerboards, CE Category A, and roughly 760L/600L tankage - are now corroborated across credible magazine sail tests (Cruising World, SAIL, Yachting World, yacht.de), a builder interview, owner accounts (including a documented Antarctic boat), and brokerage listings. The honest gaps are clearly stated rather than papered over: no published AVS or STIX, a loaded-displacement figure that varies between sources, a ballast ratio the design press has flagged as lower on the heavier 47.2, and owner-specific electrical fit-outs. Heavy-weather behaviour now rests on real firsthand accounts (50-knot Antarctic winds, hurricane-swell seakindliness) rather than engineering inference alone. Earlier fabricated or over-precise claims (a 17,000 kg loaded figure with a 'centerboard down' qualifier, an 11 cm freeboard delta, named windvane installations) have been removed.

cold_score.rationale: This is one of the strongest cold-weather boats in the comparison, and the score reflects genuine, owner-verified high-latitude credentials rather than marketing. Heating is a Refleks diesel stove with hot-water radiators and head blowers, the exact system owners single out for cruising Chile, South Georgia and Greenland. Insulation is roughly 80mm of foam with a cork layer sprayed on the aluminium frames specifically to kill thermal bridges and condensation, proven by owners in sub-10C and high-latitude winters. The hard pilothouse doghouse, with its watertight door and inside watch seat, lets the crew stand watch warm and dry, an unusually strong asset that keeps a watchkeeper functional in conditions that would exhaust an open-cockpit crew. Full collision bulkheads fore and aft and heavy bottom plating round out a boat built for cold, hostile water. The only thing holding the score short of the very top is that mast-stepping detail is not firmly documented and there is no published spec on insulated tankage, so a small margin of uncertainty remains.
cold_score.basis: Refleks heating, 80mm cork-and-foam insulation, hard watertight pilothouse, and collision bulkheads, all confirmed and owner-validated in high latitudes.

tropic_score.rationale: The boat handles the tropics competently but it is built first for cold water, and the score reflects that. The strongest tropical asset is ventilation: a large Dorade-style vent in the deckhouse keeps air moving below even with hatches shut, backed by opening pilothouse windows and good cross-flow. Refrigeration is a large standard fridge with an optional freezer, and the boat carries excellent water tankage with watermaker pre-installation, so self-sufficiency on a warm-water passage is well within reach. The marks come off for two real gaps a tropical buyer must close: cockpit shade is an optional add-on rather than a designed-in bimini, and a watermaker is an extra-cost fit rather than standard. A dark-painted aluminium hull and a heat-retaining insulated structure are assets in the cold but can make a sun-baked boat warmer below, so this is a capable tropical cruiser that needs a shade and water plan, not a purpose-built warm-water boat.
tropic_score.basis: Strong all-weather ventilation and tankage; shade and watermaker are options the tropical buyer must add.

stability_score.rationale: The boat is certified CE Category A, the highest design category for unrestricted ocean use, and its real-world behaviour in severe conditions is well regarded: reviewers and owners describe a stiff, seakindly boat that resists extra heel in gusts, and a documented owner boat has handled 50-knot Antarctic winds with an easy motion. That earns a solidly above-average score. It is held below the top, and the confidence kept moderate, for an honest reason: no Angle of Vanishing Stability, no STIX figure, and no righting-moment curve is published for this boat, so the ultimate self-righting envelope is undocumented. There is also a specific caveat worth flagging, the heavier 47.2 hull carries a lower ballast ratio than the original 47, a point the design press has raised regarding knockdown stability, which the designer answers with added hull-form stability. For a low-ballast-ratio centreboarder bound for high latitudes, a buyer should ask Boréal directly for an ISO 12217 stability calculation on the specific hull and loadout rather than rely on the Category A label alone.
stability_score.basis: CE Category A and strong real-world stiffness, offset by no published AVS/STIX and a lower ballast ratio on the heavier 47.2.

storm_verdict.text: There is now real, consistent evidence that this boat behaves well when the weather turns ugly. The Boréal 47 'Sir Ernst' has sailed to the Antarctic and crossed both the Pacific and Atlantic; in 50-knot Antarctic winds an observer described the motion as remarkably smooth and the crew as safe and relaxed, with the skipper keeping watch dry in the doghouse, and the boat's own logs note a 72-knot gust on the South Georgia to Cape Town passage. A SAIL magazine reviewer sailing in residual Hurricane Maria swell called the motion easy and forgiving with no slamming, the definition of a seakindly boat. The engineering backs this up: an ice-breaking aluminium stem, full collision bulkheads fore and aft, heavy 10mm bottom plating, a hard watertight pilothouse to keep the crew protected, and a double-headsail rig that lets the crew shorten to a deeply reefable staysail. The honest limit on this verdict is that these are firsthand seakindliness observations and high-latitude voyaging accounts, not detailed survival-storm tactical reports describing the boat lying a-hull, running off, or being knocked down, and no published stability curve exists to characterise its ultimate limit. Within those bounds, the picture is strongly positive: a proven, well-engineered high-latitude expedition boat with credible heavy-weather pedigree.

refit_projection.assumptions: These figures assume a used Boréal 47 or 47.2 roughly 5 to 12 years old being made fully passage-ready, and they are our estimates scaled to a 47-foot aluminium expedition boat, since Boréal does not publish boat-specific refit costs. The aluminium hull itself is exceptionally durable, so the spend is concentrated in three places: maintaining the corrosion-protection system that an aluminium boat lives or dies by (barrier coat, anodes, isolation of dissimilar metals), refreshing age-driven systems common to any offshore boat (rigging, sails, safety gear, electronics, energy), and adding the expedition kit that Boréal leaves as options rather than standard (watermaker, and often a lithium-and-solar energy upgrade). The engine is a long-lived Volvo Penta D2 that needs servicing, not replacing, on a boat of this age. The single most important line is corrosion discipline: caught early it is cheap, neglected it is the one thing that can seriously damage an aluminium hull, so the barrier-coat and anode work should never be deferred. Final cost depends heavily on how the individual boat was specified and cared for, so a survey by someone who knows aluminium boats is essential before purchase.

by_item.note: Inspect from 10 years; full wire and end-fitting replacement around 10 to 15 years, sooner after hard high-latitude use. Younger boats need inspection only.
Selective or full renewal for offshore reliability on a boat that has done real miles.
Refresh or replace UV-tired offshore sails; add a storm jib and trysail for high-latitude work if not already aboard.

by_item.system: Aluminium hull: epoxy barrier coat and antifoul
by_item.note: Boréal-specific priority. Barrier-coat refresh roughly every 5 to 7 years below the waterline, plus antifoul; repaint of painted topsides/deck which degrade faster than bare metal.
Consumable on a 2 to 3 year cycle. Zinc-aluminium anodes for the hull, rudder and centreboard, plus checking the hull-potential meter. Cheap, and never to be skipped on an aluminium boat.

by_item.system: Centreboard pivot, lifting gear and trunk guides
by_item.note: The one boat-specific mechanical watch item. Inspect the Ertalon pivot shaft, lifting strop and trunk guides for wear; renew as found.

by_item.system: Energy upgrade (lithium house bank and solar)
by_item.note: Convert aging AGM to LiFePO4 and add solar; recent boats run around 800 Ah lithium and roughly 1,100 W solar. Generator pre-installation supports heavy loads and a watermaker.
Watermaker is optional, not standard; pre-installation is provided. Essentially mandatory for the tropical or expedition program this boat is built for.
Upgrade or complete the offshore electronics suite; high-latitude work in particular benefits from radar and reliable satellite comms.
Expedition-grade primary and storm anchors, adequate chain, and windlass service for serious anchoring far from help.
Liferaft service or replacement on its 5 to 6 year cycle, plus EPIRB battery, flares, jacklines, tethers and harnesses.

provisioning_specific.item: Marine aluminium offcuts (matching 5083/5086 alloy), aluminium-compatible epoxy filler and zinc-spray primer
provisioning_specific.why: An aluminium boat is repaired by welding, not fibreglass. Carrying offcuts in the same alloy as the hull lets you or a local welder make a proper field repair after a grounding, dock strike or ice contact, and primer and filler stop corrosion at any fresh scrape.

provisioning_specific.item: Zinc-aluminium sacrificial anode set (hull plate, rudder and centreboard anodes)
provisioning_specific.why: The hull's galvanic protection depends on these and the hull anode consumes fastest. Correct zinc-aluminium anodes, not pure zinc which can over-protect aluminium in warm saltwater, are hard to find in remote ports, so carry a full spare set.

provisioning_specific.item: Refleks stove service kit (burner pot, door and chimney seals, jet, flue brush)
provisioning_specific.why: The Refleks diesel heater is the heart of cold-weather comfort and the part owners praise most for high-latitude cruising. Spares are Scandinavian-sourced and unavailable in most Pacific or Southern Ocean ports, so a full kit belongs aboard for any cold-water trip.

provisioning_specific.item: Centreboard spares: pivot shaft/bushing material (Ertalon or UHMW), spare lifting strop and trunk guide plates
provisioning_specific.why: Centreboard trunk and pivot wear is the one boat-specific mechanical watch item. The shaft runs on engineering plastic and the lifting gear works under load, so carrying bushing stock, a spare strop and guide material lets you fix board binding without hauling out.

provisioning_specific.item: Daggerboard adjustment fittings and locking pins (matched set)
provisioning_specific.why: The boat's superb hands-off steering and helm balance depend on the aft daggerboards. Their locking pins and adjustment cleats are small, boat-specific and not stocked in chandleries, so spares preserve your ability to trim the helm offshore.

provisioning_specific.item: Profurl furler foil section plus bearing and bushing kit
provisioning_specific.why: Both headsails furl on Profurl gear and a dented foil section can jam a sail. Sections are replaceable but come from Profurl in France, so one spare section per furler aboard prevents a stuck headsail in heavy weather.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo Penta D2 service kit (impeller, oil and fuel filters, belts, coolant, injector seals, spare zincs)
provisioning_specific.why: The D2 is well supported in major ports but not remote ones, and it also matters for charging and for feeding the heater fuel system. A full 500-hour service kit aboard avoids being engine-down in an anchorage with no chandlery.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker pre-filters and a spare membrane (if a watermaker is fitted)
provisioning_specific.why: On the months-of-autonomy program these boats are built for, the watermaker is critical and its consumables fail first. Pre-filters and a spare membrane keep fresh water flowing far from any service agent.

provisioning_specific.item: Pilothouse door and hatch gasket material (EPDM or neoprene to suit the doghouse door profile)
provisioning_specific.why: The watertight doghouse door is the boat's primary dry, warm watch position, and a failed gasket lets weather into the heated space. EPDM ages and degrades in UV, so spare seal material is cheap insurance for a long high-latitude voyage.
